# /api-contract-review

Reviews a new or modified API contract against its consumers for backward-compatibility breaks, versioning gaps, and error-contract ambiguity - the specific things that quietly break API consumers even when the change looks small.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Before merging a change to a public or internal API endpoint's request/response shape, or before publishing a new endpoint, especially when there are external or cross-team consumers who won't necessarily read the changelog before their integration breaks.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the review (e.g. "are there external/third-party consumers of this endpoint, or only internal services you control the deploy timing of?").
- The old contract (if this is a change) and the new contract - request shape, response shape, status codes, headers
- Known consumers of this API and whether they're internal (you control both sides' deploy timing) or external (you don't)
- The API's versioning scheme, if one exists (URL versioning, header versioning, no formal scheme)
- Whether this is a new endpoint or a change to an existing one
- Any existing consumer contract tests or schema validation already in place

## Workflow

1. Diff the old and new contract field by field, and classify each change as additive (safe: new optional field, new optional endpoint), or potentially breaking (field removed, field type changed, field made required that wasn't, enum value removed, semantic meaning of an existing field changed without a name/type change) - this last category, changing meaning without changing the visible contract, is the one generic schema diffing tools miss.
2. Check required vs. optional status carefully in both directions - adding a new required request field breaks existing callers even though "adding a field" sounds additive; making a previously required response field optional breaks callers relying on its presence even though "removing a requirement" sounds safe.
3. Check status code and error-shape consistency - does this change alter which status codes are returned for existing failure conditions, or change the error response body shape, since consumers often hard-code error handling against the previous shape.
4. Assess consumer blast radius realistically - an internal-only endpoint where you control both deploy timings can tolerate a coordinated breaking change; an external or multi-team-owned consumer cannot, and needs either a new version or a compatibility shim.
5. For anything flagged breaking, recommend the specific mitigation: version the endpoint, add the new field as optional with a deprecation path for the old one, or run a dual-write/dual-support period - rather than a blanket "this is breaking, don't do it," which isn't actionable.
6. Check pagination, sorting, and default-value behavior specifically if the endpoint returns a collection - silent changes to default page size, default sort order, or filtering behavior are a common source of consumer-visible breakage that doesn't show up in a naive schema diff.
7. Confirm the change is reflected in whatever contract-testing or schema-validation mechanism exists (OpenAPI spec, GraphQL schema, protobuf definitions) so the written contract doesn't drift from the actual deployed behavior.

## Output format

**API Contract Review - [Endpoint/Service] - [Date]**
- Contract Diff table: `Field/Behavior | Old | New | Classification (additive/breaking/ambiguous)`
- Breaking Changes Detail (each with the specific consumer impact and recommended mitigation)
- Error Contract Check (status codes / error shape changes, if any)
- Collection Behavior Check (pagination/sort/filter defaults, if applicable)
- Consumer Blast Radius (internal-only / external / mixed, and what that implies for rollout approach)
- Schema/Spec Sync Status (does the formal contract definition match this change)

## Do
- Check both directions of required/optional changes - adding a required field and removing a required guarantee are both breaking, in different ways.
- Flag semantic-meaning changes to unchanged-looking fields - the hardest category for naive diffing to catch.
- Give every breaking change a specific, actionable mitigation, not just a warning label.

## Don't
- Don't classify a change as "safe" purely because no field was removed - required-field additions and meaning changes break consumers just as surely.
- Don't skip the blast-radius assessment; the same technical change can be fine internally and disastrous for external consumers.
- Don't treat this as done once the endpoint code changes - verify the formal spec/schema was updated too.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Connector access verified before claiming results (if applicable)
- [ ] Approval gate present for any risky/irreversible action (if applicable)
- [ ] Every field/behavior marked "additive" was checked against both the required/optional direction and possible semantic-meaning changes, not just presence/absence
```

# /architecture-decision-record

Produces a real Architecture Decision Record for a specific technical choice - including the alternatives that were seriously considered and rejected, with the actual trade-off reasoning, not just a write-up of the decision that was made.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A team just made (or is about to make) a consequential technical decision - a database choice, a service boundary, sync vs. async messaging, build vs. buy - and needs it written down in a way that will still make sense to someone reading it in two years who's wondering "why didn't we just do the obvious thing."

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the record (e.g. "was this decision already made, or are you still deciding and want the ADR to force the comparison?").
- The decision being documented and the specific problem/context that made a decision necessary
- The alternatives that were actually considered (not a padded list - only options someone seriously evaluated)
- The constraints that mattered most in this specific decision (team size/expertise, existing infra, cost ceiling, latency/consistency requirements, timeline)
- The decision status (proposed, accepted, superseded - and if superseded, by what)
- Who has authority to accept/reverse this decision, if relevant to your team's process

## Workflow

1. State the problem in terms of the forces at play, not just "we needed a database" - name the actual constraint tension (e.g., "we need strong consistency for balance updates but the team has no ops capacity for a self-managed cluster") since that tension is what actually drove the choice.
2. List every alternative that was genuinely considered, each with its own real trade-off - not a strawman list where every option except the winner is obviously bad; if an alternative doesn't have a genuine case for it, it shouldn't be in the ADR.
3. For the chosen option, state the deciding factor explicitly - the one or two constraints that tipped it, since "it was better overall" is not reconstructable later but "we don't have the ops headcount to run a self-hosted cluster" is.
4. Write down the consequences honestly, including the negative ones being accepted knowingly (a real ADR admits "this means we give up X") - an ADR that only lists upsides of the chosen option isn't a decision record, it's marketing copy.
5. Note what would trigger revisiting this decision (a specific scale threshold, a team change, a cost trigger) so future readers know whether their situation invalidates the original reasoning.
6. Keep the record scoped to one decision - if the discussion naturally splits into two separate decisions, write two ADRs, cross-linked, rather than one bloated document.

## Output format

**ADR-[number]: [Decision Title]**
- Status: Proposed / Accepted / Superseded (by ADR-X if applicable)
- Context (the forces/constraints that made a decision necessary - 2-4 sentences)
- Alternatives Considered table: `Option | Real Case For It | Why Rejected`
- Decision (the chosen option, stated plainly)
- Deciding Factor (the specific constraint(s) that tipped it - 1-2 sentences)
- Consequences (both the accepted upsides and the knowingly accepted downsides)
- Revisit Triggers (what would make this worth reconsidering)

## Do
- Give every listed alternative a genuine case for it - a one-sided ADR is worthless to a future reader trying to understand the real trade-off.
- State the actual deciding factor in specific, falsifiable terms, not vague "best overall fit" language.
- Name the downsides being accepted, not just the upsides of the winner.

## Don't
- Don't write an ADR that reads as post-hoc justification for a decision already made for unstated reasons (office politics, sunk cost) - if that's really what happened, name it honestly in the context section.
- Don't bundle multiple independent decisions into one ADR just because they were discussed in the same meeting.
- Don't skip the revisit-triggers section - without it, ADRs calcify into decisions nobody feels empowered to reopen even when circumstances have clearly changed.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Connector access verified before claiming results (if applicable)
- [ ] Approval gate present for any risky/irreversible action (if applicable)
- [ ] Every rejected alternative has a genuine, non-strawman case stated for it before being rejected
```

# /code-review-checklist-by-language

Builds a code review checklist targeted at the specific bug classes and idioms that actually cause problems in the language/framework of the PR being reviewed, instead of a generic style-and-comments checklist.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

You're reviewing a PR (or standardizing what your team checks in review) in a language/framework where you know generic checklists miss the real failure modes - e.g., a Go PR needs a goroutine-leak and error-wrapping check that a generic checklist won't mention; a React PR needs a hook-dependency-array check that doesn't apply to backend code at all.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the checklist (e.g. "is this service handling concurrent requests, or is it a single-threaded batch job?").
- The language, framework, and version in use (Go 1.2x, Python async vs. sync, React with hooks vs. class components, etc.)
- The category of code being reviewed (API handler, background worker, UI component, data pipeline) since failure modes differ by category even within one language
- Any past incident pattern specific to this codebase worth checking for again (a recurring bug class this team has been bitten by before)
- Whether this review is pre-merge gate-level (must catch everything) or a lighter peer-review pass

## Workflow

1. Identify the language/framework-specific bug classes that generic style checklists miss entirely - for example: Go - unchecked error returns, goroutine leaks from missing context cancellation, mutex copy-by-value bugs; Python - mutable default arguments, async functions called without await silently returning a coroutine, exception swallowing in broad except blocks; JavaScript/TypeScript - floating promises, `this` binding issues, `any` type leaking past a boundary meant to be typed; React - missing/incorrect hook dependency arrays, state updates in render, key prop misuse in lists; SQL-adjacent code - N+1 query patterns, missing transaction boundaries around multi-step writes.
2. Layer in the code-category-specific checks on top of the language checks - an API handler needs input validation and auth-boundary checks; a background worker needs idempotency and retry-safety checks; a UI component needs accessibility and re-render-cost checks.
3. Add a concurrency/async-specific check only when the code category actually involves it (skip this section entirely for genuinely single-threaded, synchronous code - a padded checklist item nobody can act on is worse than no item).
4. Include exactly one or two items on error handling specificity for this language (how errors should be wrapped/propagated/logged idiomatically) since generic "handle errors" advice is functionally useless without language-specific idiom.
5. Add a resource-lifecycle check appropriate to the language (connection/file handle closing, context cancellation, cleanup in defer/finally blocks) since resource leaks are a top recurring bug class across nearly every language but the exact mechanism differs.
6. Keep the final checklist short enough to actually get used in review - 8-15 items max, each phrased as a specific yes/no check, not a vague principle.

## Output format

**Code Review Checklist - [Language/Framework] - [Code Category]**
- Language-Specific Bug Classes (checklist items, each phrased as a specific check, e.g. "Does every goroutine have a clear termination path via context or channel close?")
- Code-Category-Specific Checks (API/worker/UI/pipeline-specific items)
- Error Handling Idiom Check (1-2 items specific to this language's conventions)
- Resource Lifecycle Check (1-2 items)
- Skip List (explicitly note any category - e.g. concurrency - intentionally omitted because it doesn't apply here, so reviewers don't waste time hunting for it)

## Do
- Name the specific bug pattern in each checklist item (e.g. "mutable default argument" not "check for bugs") so a reviewer knows exactly what to look for.
- Tailor items to the actual code category, not just the language, since an API handler and a UI component in the same language need different checks.
- Keep the list short enough that reviewers will actually use it every time.

## Don't
- Don't include generic items ("code should be readable," "add comments where needed") that apply equally to every language - they add length without adding signal.
- Don't include concurrency-specific checks for genuinely single-threaded code just to fill out the list.
- Don't produce a checklist so long reviewers will skim past it; trim to the highest-value items for this specific language and category.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Connector access verified before claiming results (if applicable)
- [ ] Approval gate present for any risky/irreversible action (if applicable)
- [ ] Every checklist item names a specific bug pattern or idiom, not a generic code-quality principle
```

3. Add a concurrency/async-specific check only when the code category actually involves it (skip this section entirely for genuinely single-threaded, synchronous code - a padded checklist item nobody can act on is worse than no item).
4. Include exactly one or two items on error handling specificity for this language (how errors should be wrapped/propagated/logged idiomatically) since generic "handle errors" advice is functionally useless without language-specific idiom.
5. Add a resource-lifecycle check appropriate to the language (connection/file handle closing, context cancellation, cleanup in defer/finally blocks) since resource leaks are a top recurring bug class across nearly every language but the exact mechanism differs.
6. Keep the final checklist short enough to actually get used in review - 8-15 items max, each phrased as a specific yes/no check, not a vague principle.

## Output format

**Code Review Checklist - [Language/Framework] - [Code Category]**
- Language-Specific Bug Classes (checklist items, each phrased as a specific check, e.g. "Does every goroutine have a clear termination path via context or channel close?")
- Code-Category-Specific Checks (API/worker/UI/pipeline-specific items)
- Error Handling Idiom Check (1-2 items specific to this language's conventions)
- Resource Lifecycle Check (1-2 items)
- Skip List (explicitly note any category - e.g. concurrency - intentionally omitted because it doesn't apply here, so reviewers don't waste time hunting for it)

## Do
- Name the specific bug pattern in each checklist item (e.g. "mutable default argument" not "check for bugs") so a reviewer knows exactly what to look for.
- Tailor items to the actual code category, not just the language, since an API handler and a UI component in the same language need different checks.
- Keep the list short enough that reviewers will actually use it every time.

## Don't
- Don't include generic items ("code should be readable," "add comments where needed") that apply equally to every language - they add length without adding signal.
- Don't include concurrency-specific checks for genuinely single-threaded code just to fill out the list.
- Don't produce a checklist so long reviewers will skim past it; trim to the highest-value items for this specific language and category.

# /dependency-risk-review

Reviews a project's direct and key transitive dependencies for maintenance risk, license exposure, and version-conflict fragility, and produces a triaged list of what to act on versus what to just monitor.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Before a major release, after a supply-chain incident makes the news, when onboarding a new service with an unfamiliar dependency tree, or on a recurring cadence to catch packages that quietly went unmaintained since the last check - not a routine "run npm audit" pass, but a judgment-based review of what actually matters.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes prioritization (e.g. "is this package in the request path for production traffic, or only used in build tooling?").
- The dependency manifest (package.json/requirements.txt/Gemfile/go.mod, etc.) and lockfile if available
- Which dependencies sit in the critical path (production runtime) versus dev/build-only tooling
- The project's license posture (is this proprietary/closed-source, or does it need to stay compatible with a specific open license scheme)
- Any known incident history with a specific package, if relevant
- How much the team currently defers to automated tooling (Dependabot/Renovate) versus needs a first manual pass

## Workflow

1. Separate direct dependencies from key transitive ones early, and weight the review toward what's actually in the runtime critical path - a vulnerable dev-only linter plugin is a very different risk than a vulnerable package parsing user input in production.
2. Check maintenance health per package: time since last release, open issue/PR backlog trend, whether the maintainer count dropped to a single person (bus-factor risk), and whether there's a announced or de facto abandonment - not just "does it still have a green build badge."
3. Flag license risk specifically - copyleft licenses (GPL family) pulled into a proprietary codebase, or a license that changed between versions (some projects have re-licensed mid-stream) - since this is a legal risk category distinct from security risk and often missed by security-focused tooling.
4. Check for version-pinning fragility: dependencies pinned to a very old major version because upgrading is known to be painful, which quietly accumulates upgrade risk and blocks getting security patches for that line.
5. Look at the transitive tree for duplicate major versions of the same library pulled in by different direct dependencies - a common source of subtle runtime bugs and bundle bloat that doesn't show up in a simple vulnerability scan.
6. For anything flagged high-risk, identify a concrete next step (upgrade path, replacement candidate, vendoring the needed functionality, or "accept and monitor" with a stated reason) rather than leaving it as an unresolved red flag.
7. Triage the full findings list into "act before next release," "schedule this quarter," and "monitor only" so the team doesn't treat every finding as equally urgent.

## Output format

**Dependency Risk Review - [Project/Repo] - [Date]**
- Scope Note (what was reviewed: direct only, or direct + key transitive; runtime vs. build-time split)
- Findings table: `Package | Version | Risk Type (maintenance/license/version-conflict/security) | Severity | In Runtime Path? | Recommended Action`
- License Exposure Section (anything with copyleft or unusual terms, called out specifically)
- Bus-Factor Watchlist (single-maintainer or low-activity packages that are otherwise fine today but worth tracking)
- Triage: Act Before Next Release / Schedule This Quarter / Monitor Only

## Do
- Weight severity by whether the package sits in the production runtime path, not by vulnerability score alone.
- Treat license risk as its own category with its own action items - it's a legal exposure, not just a technical one.
- Give every high-severity finding a concrete recommended action, not just a flag.

## Don't
- Don't treat this as equivalent to an automated vulnerability scanner output - the value here is judgment about maintenance health and license risk that scanners typically miss.
- Don't recommend blanket "upgrade everything to latest" without checking for breaking changes in major version jumps.
- Don't bury license findings inside the general risk table where they're easy to skim past - they need their own section.

- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Connector access verified before claiming results (if applicable)
- [ ] Approval gate present for any risky/irreversible action (if applicable)
- [ ] Every dependency is tagged with whether it's in the production runtime path before severity is assigned
```

# /flaky-test-triage

Diagnoses the likely root-cause category behind an intermittently failing test from its failure pattern and code, and recommends a specific fix or a time-boxed quarantine - instead of the default "just re-run it" response that lets flakiness accumulate.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A test (or a backlog of several) fails intermittently - passes on re-run, fails again days later with no code change - and the team needs an actual diagnosis instead of continuing to shrug and re-run CI, which is how a codebase ends up with a permanently-ignored red suite.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the diagnosis (e.g. "does this fail more often in CI than locally, or equally in both?").
- The test code itself and the system/function it exercises
- The failure pattern: does it fail more under parallel test execution, at specific times, after specific other tests run, only in CI vs. local, or with no discernible pattern
- Recent failure logs/stack traces if available (the actual assertion that failed, not just "test failed")
- Whether the test touches shared state, time, network, randomness, or async/concurrent code
- How long this test has been flaky and how often it's currently re-run/ignored

## Workflow

1. Classify the failure pattern into a root-cause category before proposing a fix: shared/leaked state between tests (order-dependent failures), timing/race conditions (async operations not properly awaited, arbitrary sleep-based waits), external dependency flakiness (network calls, third-party services not properly mocked), environment differences (works locally, fails in CI due to resource constraints or parallelism), or non-deterministic test data (unseeded randomness, current-date dependencies).
2. Look for the specific code smell matching the suspected category - a shared mutable fixture without proper teardown, a `sleep(500)` standing in for a real wait condition, an un-mocked HTTP call, a `Date.now()` dependency with no clock injection - rather than a generic "this test is flaky" conclusion.
3. Check whether the test is asserting on an implementation detail sensitive to timing/ordering rather than the actual behavior contract, which is a design flaw in the test itself, not just an execution environment problem.
4. Distinguish "the test is wrong" (poorly written, testing something nondeterministic by nature) from "the code under test has a real race condition" (the flakiness is revealing an actual production bug) - these require completely different fixes, and conflating them either hides a real bug or wastes effort fixing production code that was never broken.
5. Recommend a concrete fix tied to the diagnosed category (proper test isolation/teardown, replacing sleep with a poll/wait-for-condition, mocking the external dependency, seeding randomness, injecting a fake clock) rather than a generic "add retries" band-aid.
6. If a clean fix isn't feasible immediately, recommend a time-boxed quarantine (skip with a tracked ticket and an expiry date) rather than an indefinite retry-until-green policy, which is how flaky tests silently stop providing any signal at all.

## Output format

**Flaky Test Triage - [Test Name/File]**
- Failure Pattern Observed (what conditions correlate with failure)
- Root Cause Category (shared state / timing-race / external dependency / environment / non-determinism)
- Specific Code Smell Identified (the actual line/pattern causing it)
- Is This a Real Production Bug or a Test Design Flaw? (stated explicitly)
- Recommended Fix (concrete, tied to root cause)
- If No Immediate Fix: Quarantine Recommendation (skip + ticket + expiry date, not indefinite)

## Do
- Name the specific root-cause category and code smell, not just "the test is flaky."
- Explicitly determine whether the flakiness reveals a real bug in the code under test versus a poorly written test - this changes who should fix what.
- Prefer a time-boxed quarantine with a tracked ticket over an indefinite retry-and-ignore policy.

## Don't
- Don't recommend "just add a retry/rerun" as the fix - it treats the symptom and lets the real cause compound over time.
- Don't quarantine a test without an expiry date and an owner - untracked skips are how test suites silently stop testing things.
- Don't assume every flaky test is a test problem; check whether it's surfacing a genuine race condition in production code first.

- [ ] Connector access verified before claiming results (if applicable)
- [ ] Approval gate present for any risky/irreversible action (if applicable)
- [ ] The diagnosis states plainly whether this is a real bug in the code under test or a flaw in the test itself
```

# /incident-postmortem-writeup

Writes a blameless postmortem for a production incident that pushes past "human error" or "bad luck" to the actual systemic contributing factors, and produces action items specific enough that someone can be held to completing them.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

An incident just got resolved (an outage, a data issue, a security near-miss) and needs a written postmortem - for internal learning, for a customer-facing summary, or both - before the details get fuzzy and before the natural instinct to write "we'll add more monitoring" as the entire action plan takes over.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the analysis (e.g. "was there an existing alert for this condition that didn't fire, or no alert configured at all?").
- The incident timeline as known so far: detection time, key events, mitigation steps, resolution time
- What actually triggered the incident (the proximate cause) and, separately, anyone's account of a decision or action that preceded it
- What existing safeguards (tests, alerts, review processes, rate limits) were supposed to catch this and didn't, or weren't in place
- Customer/business impact: duration, scope (how many users/how much data/revenue), and whether it's customer-facing enough to need external comms
- Whether this is a first occurrence or a repeat of a similar past incident

## Workflow

1. Build the factual timeline first, strictly chronological and free of blame language, distinguishing what's confirmed (from logs/metrics) from what's reported/recalled (from people's memory), since these have different reliability.
2. Identify the proximate cause (the immediate technical trigger) clearly, then explicitly push one level deeper to the contributing factors - the missing test, the alert threshold set too loose, the deploy process that allowed an unreviewed change, the documentation gap that led to a reasonable-seeming but wrong assumption - because stopping at the proximate cause is how the same incident recurs in a different form six months later.
3. If a human action is part of the proximate cause, reframe it as a systemic question by default: not "why did the engineer make this mistake" but "what made this mistake easy to make, and what would have caught it before it reached production" - this is the actual mechanism of a blameless postmortem, not just a tone choice.
4. Name what worked, not only what failed - the alert that did fire correctly, the runbook step that worked as designed, the person who caught it faster than expected - because postmortems that are 100% failure-focused undercount what to preserve and make the exercise feel purely punitive, which discourages honest incident reporting next time.
5. Write action items that are specific and ownable - a named owner, a concrete deliverable, and a due date - and explicitly reject vague action items like "improve monitoring" or "add more tests" unless they specify exactly what signal/test and why it would have caught this specific failure mode.
6. Classify each action item by what it actually prevents: fully prevents recurrence, reduces detection time, reduces blast radius, or reduces recovery time - since "prevent this exact thing from ever happening again" isn't always achievable, and the postmortem should be honest about which category each fix falls into.
7. If this is a repeat of a similar past incident, say so explicitly and treat that as the most urgent finding in the whole document - a recurring incident with a previously-written but unexecuted action item is a process failure in its own right.

## Output format

**Incident Postmortem - [Incident Name/ID] - [Date] - Severity: [Sev level]**
- Impact Summary (duration, scope, customer/business impact)
- Timeline (chronological, tagged confirmed vs. reported)
- Proximate Cause
- Contributing Factors (the systemic layer beneath the proximate cause)
- What Worked (safeguards/responses that functioned as intended)
- Repeat Incident Check (Y/N - if yes, link to prior postmortem and note why the prior fix didn't hold)
- Action Items table: `Action | Owner | Due Date | Prevents/Reduces (recurrence/detection time/blast radius/recovery time)`

## Do
- Push past the proximate cause to systemic contributing factors every time.
- Reframe any human-action finding as a systemic question about what made the mistake easy to make.

# /incident-postmortem-writeup

Writes a blameless postmortem for a production incident that pushes past "human error" or "bad luck" to the actual systemic contributing factors, and produces action items specific enough that someone can be held to completing them.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

An incident just got resolved (an outage, a data issue, a security near-miss) and needs a written postmortem - for internal learning, for a customer-facing summary, or both - before the details get fuzzy and before the natural instinct to write "we'll add more monitoring" as the entire action plan takes over.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the analysis (e.g. "was there an existing alert for this condition that didn't fire, or no alert configured at all?").
- The incident timeline as known so far: detection time, key events, mitigation steps, resolution time
- What actually triggered the incident (the proximate cause) and, separately, anyone's account of a decision or action that preceded it
- What existing safeguards (tests, alerts, review processes, rate limits) were supposed to catch this and didn't, or weren't in place
- Customer/business impact: duration, scope (how many users/how much data/revenue), and whether it's customer-facing enough to need external comms
- Whether this is a first occurrence or a repeat of a similar past incident

## Workflow

1. Build the factual timeline first, strictly chronological and free of blame language, distinguishing what's confirmed (from logs/metrics) from what's reported/recalled (from people's memory), since these have different reliability.
2. Identify the proximate cause (the immediate technical trigger) clearly, then explicitly push one level deeper to the contributing factors - the missing test, the alert threshold set too loose, the deploy process that allowed an unreviewed change, the documentation gap that led to a reasonable-seeming but wrong assumption - because stopping at the proximate cause is how the same incident recurs in a different form six months later.
3. If a human action is part of the proximate cause, reframe it as a systemic question by default: not "why did the engineer make this mistake" but "what made this mistake easy to make, and what would have caught it before it reached production" - this is the actual mechanism of a blameless postmortem, not just a tone choice.
4. Name what worked, not only what failed - the alert that did fire correctly, the runbook step that worked as designed, the person who caught it faster than expected - because postmortems that are 100% failure-focused undercount what to preserve and make the exercise feel purely punitive, which discourages honest incident reporting next time.
5. Write action items that are specific and ownable - a named owner, a concrete deliverable, and a due date - and explicitly reject vague action items like "improve monitoring" or "add more tests" unless they specify exactly what signal/test and why it would have caught this specific failure mode.
6. Classify each action item by what it actually prevents: fully prevents recurrence, reduces detection time, reduces blast radius, or reduces recovery time - since "prevent this exact thing from ever happening again" isn't always achievable, and the postmortem should be honest about which category each fix falls into.
7. If this is a repeat of a similar past incident, say so explicitly and treat that as the most urgent finding in the whole document - a recurring incident with a previously-written but unexecuted action item is a process failure in its own right.

## Output format

**Incident Postmortem - [Incident Name/ID] - [Date] - Severity: [Sev level]**
- Impact Summary (duration, scope, customer/business impact)
- Timeline (chronological, tagged confirmed vs. reported)
- Proximate Cause
- Contributing Factors (the systemic layer beneath the proximate cause)
- What Worked (safeguards/responses that functioned as intended)
- Repeat Incident Check (Y/N - if yes, link to prior postmortem and note why the prior fix didn't hold)
- Action Items table: `Action | Owner | Due Date | Prevents/Reduces (recurrence/detection time/blast radius/recovery time)`

## Do
- Push past the proximate cause to systemic contributing factors every time.
- Reframe any human-action finding as a systemic question about what made the mistake easy to make.
- Give every action item a named owner, a due date, and a specific prevention mechanism.

## Don't
- Don't let "human error" stand as a final answer - it's always a symptom of a system that allowed the error to reach production.
- Don't accept vague action items like "add more monitoring" without specifying the exact signal and why it would have caught this failure.
- Don't skip checking for a prior similar incident - a silent repeat is often the most important finding in the document.

- [ ] Approval gate present for any risky/irreversible action (if applicable)

# /migration-runbook

Produces an execution-ready runbook for a data, schema, or service migration, with a rollback plan attached to every stage - not just a high-level plan of steps, but the specific commands, checks, and abort conditions an on-call engineer could follow under pressure.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

You're about to execute a migration with real blast radius - a schema change on a live production table, cutting a service over to a new data store, moving traffic between infrastructure - and need a runbook precise enough that someone other than the author (or the author, at 2am under pressure) can execute it correctly and know exactly when to stop and roll back.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the plan materially (e.g. "can this migration run with the old and new systems both live simultaneously, or does it require a hard cutover window?").
- What's migrating (schema change, data backfill, service/infra cutover) and the before/after state
- Whether dual-write/dual-read or a blue-green approach is possible, or if this requires a hard cutover window
- The rollback mechanism available at each stage (can data changes be reversed, is there a snapshot/backup point, can traffic be routed back)
- Expected duration and blast radius if something goes wrong mid-migration (how many users/systems affected, is data loss possible)
- Who needs to be on-call/notified during the window, and any maintenance-window or approval requirement

## Safety

- Propose before acting: present the full runbook for review and explicit sign-off before any step is executed against production.
- Never take an irreversible action (dropping a column, deleting old infrastructure, disabling the rollback path) without the user explicitly confirming the migration is verified successful and stable.

## Workflow

1. State the pre-migration state and the target end-state explicitly, including exactly what "done and safe to consider complete" looks like - a runbook without a clear finish line tends to leave rollback scaffolding in place forever or get torn down too early.
2. Break the migration into stages where each stage individually is reversible, rather than one large irreversible leap - e.g., add-new-column-nullable → backfill → dual-write → switch reads → remove old column, instead of a single "migrate the schema" step.
3. At every stage, define the verification check that confirms the stage succeeded before proceeding (row count match, checksum comparison, a specific smoke test) - proceeding on assumption instead of verification is the most common cause of migrations that fail silently partway through.
4. At every stage, define the specific rollback action and its own time cost/risk - some stages roll back instantly (revert a config flag), others take real time (restore from snapshot) - and note which stages, once passed, are effectively a point of no return, flagged explicitly.
5. Identify the load-bearing assumption that, if wrong, breaks the whole plan (e.g., "we assume no writes happen to the old table during the backfill window") and state how it's being enforced or monitored, not just hoped for.
6. Write the abort criteria concretely - the specific error rate, latency threshold, or verification failure that means "stop and roll back now" rather than leaving that judgment call to be made under pressure in the moment.
7. Include a post-migration cleanup step with its own explicit trigger condition (e.g., "remove old table only after N days of stable dual-write with zero discrepancies") so temporary safety scaffolding doesn't get torn down prematurely - or forgotten and left as permanent cruft.

## Output format

**Migration Runbook - [Migration Name] - [Date/Window]**
- Pre-State / Target End-State (explicit)
- Load-Bearing Assumptions (what must hold true, and how it's monitored/enforced)
- Stages table: `Stage | Action (specific commands/steps) | Verification Check | Rollback Action | Point of No Return? (Y/N)`
- Abort Criteria (concrete thresholds that trigger a stop-and-rollback decision)
- On-Call/Notification Plan (who's watching, who's notified, escalation path)
- Post-Migration Cleanup (what gets removed, and the explicit trigger condition for when it's safe to remove)

## Do
- Break the migration into individually reversible stages rather than one big irreversible jump.
- Give every stage both a verification check and a rollback action, not just a description of what the stage does.
- State abort criteria as concrete thresholds, decided in advance, not left as an in-the-moment judgment call.

## Don't
- Don't execute anything against production from this runbook without explicit sign-off - draft and propose only.
- Don't mark any stage "reversible" without a concretely tested or at least clearly described rollback mechanism.
- Don't leave cleanup of temporary migration scaffolding (dual-write code, old tables) without an explicit trigger condition - it either gets removed too early or lingers as permanent debt.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Connector access verified before claiming results (if applicable)
- [ ] Approval gate present for any risky/irreversible action (if applicable)
- [ ] Every stage marked as a "point of no return" is explicitly flagged as such before the runbook is considered ready to execute
```

# /technical-debt-inventory

Builds a technical debt inventory where every item is tied to a concrete cost it's currently imposing (velocity drag, incident risk, onboarding friction) so the list can actually compete for prioritization against feature work, instead of sitting as an ignored wishlist.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Ahead of a planning cycle where tech debt needs to compete for a slot against feature work, when a new engineer's onboarding pain surfaces old shortcuts nobody documented, or periodically to keep debt visible before it causes an incident that makes the case involuntarily.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes prioritization (e.g. "has any of this debt already caused a production incident, or is the cost so far only velocity drag?").
- The codebase areas or systems to inventory (whole system, or a specific service/module under review)
- Known pain points already voiced by the team (slow areas, "don't touch that file," recurring workarounds)
- Recent incident history, if any items on the list have already caused production problems
- Upcoming roadmap items that might be blocked or slowed by specific debt items (this is often the strongest prioritization signal)
- Team size/capacity available for debt paydown, roughly, so recommendations are realistic

## Workflow

1. For each debt item, state the concrete mechanism of cost it currently imposes - "this module has no tests, so every change to it takes 3x longer because engineers manually verify by hand" is inventory-worthy; "this code isn't very clean" is not, and should be pushed back on until a real cost is named.
2. Separate debt by cost type: velocity drag (slows every future change in this area), incident risk (has caused or could plausibly cause an outage/data issue), onboarding friction (new engineers consistently get stuck here), and scaling ceiling (works fine now, will break at a specific known threshold) - since these compete for prioritization differently and get funded by different arguments.
3. Check each item against the upcoming roadmap - debt that blocks or will be touched by a planned feature deserves priority regardless of its abstract severity, because paying it down now is cheaper than paying it down mid-feature under deadline pressure.
4. Estimate rough paydown effort per item (hours/days, not false-precision story points) so cost-versus-effort can actually be compared across the list.
5. Flag any item with real incident history or a known scaling ceiling with a date/threshold attached (e.g., "this breaks at ~10x current data volume, currently at 3x") as high-priority regardless of how annoying it is day-to-day, since these have a forcing function that pure velocity-drag items don't.
6. Explicitly reject vague entries during the inventory pass ("this code is ugly," "we should refactor this someday") unless a real cost can be attached - a debt inventory that includes unfounded entries loses credibility with whoever has to approve funding it.
7. Rank the final list by cost-to-leave-unaddressed versus effort-to-fix, not by how long an item has been on the backlog or how loudly one engineer advocates for it.

## Output format

**Technical Debt Inventory - [System/Codebase] - [Date]**
- Items table: `Item | Cost Type (velocity/incident-risk/onboarding/scaling-ceiling) | Concrete Cost Mechanism | Roadmap Collision? (Y/N + which item) | Est. Paydown Effort | Priority`
- Incident-History-Linked Items (called out separately, with dates)
- Scaling-Ceiling Items (with the known threshold and current position relative to it)
- Rejected/Vague Entries (items considered but not included because no concrete cost could be attached, for transparency)
- Top 3 Recommended for Next Planning Cycle (with the specific business case for each)

## Do
- Require a concrete cost mechanism for every entry - reject vague "this is messy" items unless a real cost can be named.
- Cross-check every item against the upcoming roadmap; debt that blocks planned work has the strongest case for prioritization.
- Give scaling-ceiling items a specific threshold and current position, not just a vague "this won't scale" warning.

## Don't
- Don't let this become a dumping ground for every engineer's pet annoyance without a cost attached - that's what kills a debt inventory's credibility in planning meetings.
- Don't rank by backlog age or advocacy volume instead of actual cost-versus-effort.
- Don't omit items with real incident history even if they seem "already handled" - recurrence risk is exactly what a debt inventory should track.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Connector access verified before claiming results (if applicable)
- [ ] Approval gate present for any risky/irreversible action (if applicable)
- [ ] Every included item has a stated concrete cost mechanism; anything without one was moved to the rejected/vague list instead
```

# /test-plan-from-spec

Converts a feature spec or PRD into a requirement-by-requirement test plan, surfacing the untested edge cases and ambiguous acceptance criteria the spec left implicit.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A spec or PRD just landed for a feature that needs test coverage before it ships, and you need a plan that a QA engineer (or the dev writing their own tests) can execute against - not a restatement of the spec, but the actual test matrix including the failure modes the spec didn't spell out.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes coverage scope (e.g. "is this feature behind a flag, and do both flag states need coverage?").
- The spec/PRD text itself, including acceptance criteria if stated
- The system's existing behavior this feature changes or replaces (so regression risk is scoped, not guessed)
- Which layer(s) need coverage - unit, integration, end-to-end, manual exploratory - and any existing test infrastructure to target
- Known integration points (external APIs, other services, feature flags) that the spec references but doesn't fully define behavior for
- Risk tolerance/timeline (a hotfix needs a leaner plan than a new core feature)

## Workflow

1. Extract every discrete, testable requirement from the spec as its own line - split compound requirements ("user can edit and delete their profile") into separate testable units, since bundled requirements hide missing coverage.
2. For each requirement, identify the acceptance criteria as written, and separately flag any requirement where the spec states the happy path but is silent on error/failure behavior - this silence is the most common source of production bugs, not the happy path itself.
3. Derive edge cases systematically per requirement type: boundary values for anything numeric/length-based, empty/null/malformed input, concurrent/race conditions for anything stateful, permission/role variations for anything access-controlled, and the specific integration failure modes for anything depending on an external system.
4. Flag ambiguous acceptance criteria explicitly as open questions for the spec author rather than guessing at intended behavior and writing a test case against your own assumption.
5. Map each test case to the most appropriate layer (unit for logic branches, integration for cross-component behavior, E2E for user-facing flows) instead of defaulting everything to manual/E2E, which is slower and more brittle than necessary.
6. Identify regression risk areas - existing functionality this feature could break - based on what the spec says it changes, and add explicit regression test cases for those, not just new-feature cases.
7. Prioritize the test cases (must-pass-before-ship vs. nice-to-have coverage) so a time-constrained QA pass still catches the highest-risk gaps first.

## Output format

**Test Plan - [Feature Name] - from [Spec/PRD reference]**
- Requirements Traceability table: `Requirement | Acceptance Criteria (as written) | Spec Silent On? (Y/N + what)`
- Test Cases table: `ID | Requirement | Layer (unit/integration/E2E/manual) | Case Description | Priority (must-pass/should-pass/nice-to-have)`
- Edge Cases Derived (not explicit in spec, grouped by requirement)
- Regression Risk Areas (existing behavior this feature could break, with test case IDs)
- Open Questions for Spec Author (ambiguous acceptance criteria, numbered)

## Do
- Split compound requirements into individually testable units before deriving test cases.
- Flag spec silence on error paths as explicitly as you flag stated requirements - it's where bugs actually hide.
- Route each test case to the cheapest layer that can actually catch the bug (unit over E2E wherever the logic allows it).

## Don't
- Don't write a test case against a guessed interpretation of ambiguous acceptance criteria - log it as an open question instead.
- Don't default every test case to end-to-end/manual; that inflates cost and slows the suite without improving actual defect detection.
- Don't skip regression risk just because the spec only describes new behavior - new features break old ones more often than specs admit.

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Connector access verified before claiming results (if applicable)
- [ ] Approval gate present for any risky/irreversible action (if applicable)
- [ ] Every requirement where the spec is silent on error/failure behavior is explicitly flagged, not silently filled in with an assumption
